title: Tensors of Minimal Border Rank
speaker: Joseph Landsberg
abstract: A class of tensors, called "concise (m,m,m)-tensors  of minimal border rank", play an important role in proving upper bounds for the complexity of matrix multiplication. For that reason Problem 15.2 of "Algebraic Complexity Theory" by Bürgisser, Clausen and Shokrollahi is to classify tensors of minimal border rank. It is now now understood that this question is equivalent to notoriously difficult questions in algebraic geometry and commutative algebra. However the full question is not what is needed for complexity theory. I will explain recent progress on the question in several directions. A surprising role is played by degree seven zero dimensional local Gorenstein schemes.

proof_stages.label: Border rank, Kronecker powers, and the laser-method mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up tensors over complex vector spaces, border rank, the matrix multiplication tensor and exponent omega, Kronecker products/powers, degeneration, and the basic laser-method implication that a degeneration of a Kronecker power to matrix multiplication gives an upper bound on omega.

proof_stages.label: Coppersmith-Winograd benchmark and asymptotic value barrier
proof_stages.summary: The big Coppersmith-Winograd tensor is introduced as the long-standing input to laser-method upper bounds, then conciseness, minimal border rank, asymptotic rank, asymptotic subrank, and irreversibility are used to explain why one must either improve the method or find a tensor with better asymptotic value.

proof_stages.label: Geometric hunting ground in the boundary of the secant variety
proof_stages.summary: Using reductive group orbit closures and Matsushima-type boundary purity, the speaker identifies concise tensors of minimal border rank as the relevant boundary of the secant variety, recalls the small-dimension boundary classification, and states that for m at least 16 new, non-orbit-closure boundary components exist.

proof_stages.label: Genericity conditions and reduction to algebra structure tensors
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines one-generic, binding, and generic tensors, notes that one-generic minimal-border-rank tensors degenerate to Coppersmith-Winograd and hence are no worse for the laser method, and then uses binding to identify such tensors with structure tensors of unital associative commutative algebras, Gorenstein in the one-generic case.

proof_stages.label: Apolar Gorenstein algebras and smoothability criterion
proof_stages.summary: Finite-dimensional graded Gorenstein algebras are constructed from homogeneous polynomials by quotienting the dual symmetric algebra by the apolar annihilator, and their structure tensors are shown to have minimal border rank exactly when the algebras are smoothable in the Hilbert scheme, with cubic seven-variable cases smoothable.

proof_stages.label: New boundary component and explicit higher-value tensor
proof_stages.summary: For m=16 the cubic Gorenstein construction yields a new component of the boundary of the minimal-border-rank variety, and normal forms lead already at n=3 to an explicit tensor of the form CW6 plus an extra cubic term, with the same cost but strictly larger asymptotic subrank than CW6.

proof_stages.label: Why the added tensor should improve the laser method
proof_stages.summary: In questions after the main talk, the speaker explains the block-matrix normal form in which an extra symmetric tensor fills an unused block at no border-rank cost, the need to separate its contribution in high Kronecker powers, and the known estimates proving the asymptotic subrank advantage is strict.

examples.name: Small Coppersmith-Winograd tensor as precursor
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker recalls that the small Coppersmith-Winograd tensor was the earlier state-of-the-art object and that the big tensor arose by adding terms, illustrating the ad hoc combinatorial origin rather than driving the present proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the small Coppersmith-Winograd tensor is identified as the earlier piece/precursor and later named as a summand, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it itself

examples.name: Three-by-three permanent and determinant tensors
examples.note: The permanent and determinant tensors are cited as alternate candidate high-value tensors for proving omega equals two, illustrating the strategy of increasing asymptotic subrank rather than lowering cost.
examples.engagement_reason: the 3-by-3 permanent and determinant tensors are cited with known relevance, lower-bound news, and Kronecker-square facts, but the speaker does not compute with them in this talk

examples.name: Generic Hilbert functions of apolar Gorenstein algebras
examples.note: The speaker lists the graded dimensions for generic cubic and quartic apolar quotients to make the finite-dimensional graded Gorenstein construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the apolar quotient and explicitly works out the generic graded Hilbert-function patterns for degrees three and four
